papers.title: IRAS03063+5735: A Bowshock Nebula Powered by an Early B Star
papers.author_names: Henry A. Kobulnicky Michael J. Lundquist Anirban Bhattacharjee C. R. Kerton

abstract.text: Mid-infrared images from the Spitzer Space Telescope Galactic Legacy Infrared MidPlane Survey Extraordinaire program reveal that the infrared source IRAS 03063+5735 is a bowshock nebula produced by an early B star, 2MASS 03101044+5747035. We present new optical spectra of this star, classify it as a B1.5 V, and determine a probable association with a molecular cloud complex at V_LSR=-38 -- -42 km/s in the outer Galaxy near l=140.59 degr, b=-0.250 degr. On the basis of spectroscopic parallax, we estimate a distance of 4.0 +/-1 kpc to both the bowshock nebula and the molecular complex. One plausible scenario is that this a high-velocity runaway star impinging upon a molecular cloud. We identify the HII region and stellar cluster associated with IRAS 03064+5638 at a projected distance of 64 pc as one plausible birth site. The spectrophotometric distance and linkage to a molecular feature provides another piece of data helping to secure the ill-determined rotation curve in the outer Galaxy. As a by-product of spectral typing this star, we present empirical spectral diagnostic diagrams suitable for approximate spectral classification of O and B stars using He lines in the little-used yellow-red portion of the optical spectrum.

source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section presents infrared and optical observations of IRAS 03063+5735, revealing a bilateral bowshock nebula likely powered by an early B-type star, with spectroscopic analysis confirming its spectral type and supporting its role as the progenitor of the observed emission. Analysis: The analysis of optical spectra and equivalent widths of helium and hydrogen lines indicates that Star A has a spectral type later than B0.5, consistent with a B-type star lacking the strong He II λ5411 emission characteristic of hotter, earlier-type stars. Discussion: The main conclusion is that Star A is likely a recently ejected runaway B1.5 star, not a low-velocity star, as evidenced by the absence of measurable proper motion and the morphology of the bowshock nebula, with its birthplace most likely in the H II region IRAS 03064+5638 due south, supported by coincident 12CO emission and the absence of evidence for a high-density molecular cloud or stellar cluster. Conclusions: IRAS 03063+5735 is identified as a bowshock nebula powered by a runaway B1.5 V star interacting with a molecular cloud, likely originating from a stellar cluster at 64 pc, providing new insights into star formation and galactic structure. Introduction: This paper presents the first identification of a bowshock nebula associated with an early B star in the outer Galaxy, leveraging GLIMPSE 360 mid-infrared data and optical spectroscopy to determine the star's properties, distance, and velocity, thereby solving the problem of detecting and characterizing runaway massive stars in distant regions.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper presents new optical spectra, spectral classification, distance estimate, and analysis of a specific high-velocity runaway star (2MASS 03101044+5747035) producing a bowshock nebula
